response:
The error you encounter stems from a version mismatch where your WPF project targets the legacy .NET Framework while your main application runs on modern .NET 7. WPF relies heavily on specific assembly versions that are not automatically resolved when a .NET 7 application tries to load a .NET Framework 4.0 assembly directly. This conflict causes the runtime to fail because the specific version of PresentationFramework requested does not exist in the .NET 7 environment.

To resolve this without downgrading your main application, you must ensure that your WPF project is also targeting .NET 7 instead of .NET Framework. This change allows both projects to share the same runtime and assembly versions, eliminating the need for the system to locate legacy files that are incompatible with the modern framework. You can achieve this by updating the TargetFramework property in your WPF project file to net7.0-windows.

cousin to the solution is the use of a shared library pattern where the UI logic is abstracted into a class library that does not directly depend on WPF assemblies. By moving your business logic and data models into a separate project that targets .NET Standard or .NET 7, you can reference this library from both your WPF and Cocoa implementations without triggering the assembly loading error. This architectural shift ensures that the platform-specific UI code remains isolated while the core functionality stays consistent across operating systems.

Another effective strategy involves using dependency injection to load the WPF window only when the runtime detects that the application is executing on Windows. You can create a factory method that checks the operating system and instantiates the appropriate window class, ensuring that WPF assemblies are never loaded on macOS where they are unavailable. This approach prevents the runtime from attempting to resolve PresentationFramework on platforms that do not support it, thereby avoiding the file not found exception entirely.

Finally, verify that your build configuration is set to produce a self-contained deployment or that the necessary Windows-specific dependencies are correctly included in your publish profile. If you are using a framework-dependent deployment, ensure that the target machine has the correct .NET runtime installed and that your project references are explicitly set to the .NET 7 versions of the WPF assemblies. Following these steps will allow your cross-platform application to function correctly on both Windows and macOS without requiring a regression to the older .NET Framework.
